questions.question: What were the long-term effects of the Walton & Cohen social belonging intervention?

options.text: No measurable effects — it was ineffective
Small short-term mood boost that faded within days
Significant improvement in GPA, reduced health center visits, higher well-being — effects lasting YEARS after a single 1-hour intervention
Only worked for extroverted students

questions.explanation: The Walton & Cohen intervention (reframing social adversity as 'common and transient') produced remarkable long-term effects: improved GPA (especially for minority students), fewer stress-related health center visits, and higher long-term well-being — all from a single 1-hour intervention. Why? It reduced shame and catastrophic interpretation, enabling behavioral re-engagement. This demonstrates the power of cognitive reframing for belonging.

questions.question: What is rejection sensitivity (RS)?

options.text: A personality disorder requiring medication
Anxious expectation of rejection that biases interpretation of ambiguous social signals, reducing approach behavior and maintaining avoidance
A rare condition affecting only people with trauma histories
The same as Social Anxiety Disorder

questions.explanation: Rejection sensitivity is the tendency to anxiously expect, readily perceive, and intensely react to rejection. It creates a self-fulfilling prophecy: you predict rejection → interpret ambiguous signals as negative → avoid to protect yourself → never get disconfirming evidence → belief strengthens. RS is not a disorder; it's a cognitive-emotional pattern that can be modified through attribution flexibility, behavioral experiments, and self-compassion.

questions.question: According to research, when does rejection motivate social repair (increased approach) vs. avoidance?

options.text: All rejection always causes avoidance
Moderate rejection motivates social repair; only severe/hostile rejection produces avoidance
All rejection always motivates approach
Only extroverts re-engage after rejection

questions.explanation: Research shows moderate rejection actually motivates social repair — you try harder to reconnect. Only severe or hostile rejection produces withdrawal. Most of what feels like rejection (delayed text, declined invitation, brief conversation) is mild-to-moderate. The adaptive response is re-engagement, not avoidance. Clear rejection (repeated declined invitations with no counter-offers) signals lack of fit — redirect effort elsewhere. Hostile rejection (mockery, exclusion with intent to harm) requires protective withdrawal.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 3-minute rejection recovery self-compassion practice?

options.text: To deny that rejection happened
To eliminate all pain from rejection
To reduce shame (which drives avoidance), normalize the experience, and enable behavioral re-engagement
To convince yourself that you don't need social connection

questions.explanation: The self-compassion practice has five steps: (1) acknowledge the pain, (2) normalize the experience ('everyone feels this'), (3) offer yourself kindness, (4) reframe as transient, and (5) plan behavioral re-engagement. Research shows self-compassion accelerates recovery from rejection by reducing shame. Shame drives avoidance; self-compassion enables re-engagement. You're not eliminating pain or denying rejection — you're treating yourself with the kindness that allows you to try again.

questions.question: When should you withdraw after perceived rejection rather than re-engage?

options.text: After any declined invitation
After a delayed text response
After severe/hostile rejection (mockery, exclusion with intent to harm, abusive responses) — protect yourself
Immediately, to avoid any risk of further rejection

questions.explanation: Withdraw after severe/hostile rejection: mockery, intentional exclusion to harm, or abusive responses. This isn't about 'trying harder' — it's about recognizing when a person or context is unsafe. Most perceived rejection is mild-to-moderate and warrants re-engagement (delayed texts, one declined invitation, brief awkward moments). Clear rejection (repeated declines with no counter-offers) signals lack of fit — redirect effort to others. Only hostile/harmful rejection requires protective withdrawal and seeking support.

title: Rejection Recovery and Social Courage - Quiz
